questions.questionText: Which keyword creates a block-scoped variable that cannot be reassigned?
questions.explanation: const declares a block-scoped binding that cannot be reassigned after initialization.

questions.questionText: What does typeof null return?
questions.explanation: typeof null returns "object" - a long-standing quirk in JavaScript.

questions.questionText: Which of these is a primitive data type in JavaScript?
questions.explanation: Symbol is one of JavaScript's primitive types, alongside string, number, boolean, null, undefined, and bigint.

questions.questionText: What will typeof undefined return?
questions.explanation: An unassigned variable has the type undefined.

questions.questionText: A closure is created when...
options: A function is declared using arrow syntax
A function retains access to its outer scope's variables even after the outer function has returned
A variable is declared with let inside a loop
A function is passed as a callback
questions.explanation: Closures let an inner function 'remember' the variables from the scope it was created in.

questions.questionText: function outer(){ let count=0; return function(){ count++; return count; }; } const inc = outer(); console.log(inc()); console.log(inc()); - what is logged?
questions.explanation: Each call to inc() increments the same closed-over count variable, producing 1 then 2.

questions.questionText: Closures are commonly used to implement...
questions.explanation: Closures let you keep variables private, exposing only the functions that can access them.

questions.questionText: Which statement about closures is true?
options: Closures only work with arrow functions
Each call to an outer function creates a new, independent closure scope
Closures can only be created inside classes
questions.explanation: Every invocation of the outer function creates a fresh scope, so each closure is independent.

questions.questionText: What are the three states a Promise can be in?
questions.explanation: A Promise starts pending and settles into either fulfilled or rejected.

questions.questionText: Which method is used to handle a rejected Promise?
questions.explanation: .catch() registers a callback specifically for rejection.

questions.questionText: What does Promise.all() do?
options: Resolves when the first promise settles
Resolves when all promises resolve, or rejects as soon as any one rejects
questions.explanation: Promise.all waits for every promise to fulfill, but short-circuits on the first rejection.

questions.questionText: What does .finally() do on a Promise chain?
options: Only runs if the promise is rejected
Runs regardless of whether the promise resolved or rejected
Converts the promise to synchronous code
questions.explanation: .finally() runs cleanup code no matter how the promise settles.

questions.questionText: What does the await keyword do?
options: Pauses execution of the async function until the Promise settles
Converts a callback into a promise
Runs code in a separate thread
Blocks the entire Node.js event loop
questions.explanation: await pauses only the async function itself, not the whole event loop.

questions.questionText: How do you handle errors in an async/await function?
options: You cannot handle errors with async/await
questions.explanation: try/catch around an awaited call catches rejected promises as thrown errors.

questions.questionText: What must a function be declared as to use await inside it?
questions.explanation: await is only valid inside a function marked async (or at the top level of a module).

questions.questionText: What does an async function always return?
questions.explanation: Async functions always wrap their return value in a Promise, even if you return a plain value.

questions.questionText: Between microtasks (e.g. Promise callbacks) and macrotasks (e.g. setTimeout), which run first?
options: Microtasks are processed before the next macrotask
They run in the same queue
questions.explanation: After each macrotask, the microtask queue is fully drained before the next macrotask runs.

questions.questionText: What is the call stack?
options: The structure that tracks function calls currently executing
questions.explanation: The call stack tracks the currently executing function calls, LIFO.

questions.questionText: Which of these is a macrotask?
questions.explanation: Timers like setTimeout are scheduled as macrotasks.

questions.questionText: Why is JavaScript described as single-threaded with a non-blocking model?
options: It runs on multiple CPU cores simultaneously
It uses one call stack but offloads async operations (I/O, timers) so the stack isn't blocked
It cannot run any asynchronous code
It compiles to multiple threads automatically
questions.explanation: The runtime hands off I/O and timers to the environment, freeing the single call stack.

questions.questionText: Which method creates a new array by applying a function to every element, without mutating the original?
questions.explanation: map returns a new array; it never mutates the original.

questions.questionText: What does Array.prototype.reduce() do?
options: Executes a reducer function to accumulate array values into a single result
questions.explanation: reduce folds an array down to a single accumulated value.

questions.questionText: Which array method returns a new array containing only elements that pass a test?
questions.explanation: filter keeps only the elements for which the callback returns true.

questions.questionText: What does Array.prototype.find() return?
options: The index of the first match
The first element that satisfies the test function
questions.explanation: find returns the first matching element itself, or undefined if none match.

questions.questionText: JSX is...
options: A templating engine unique to Vue
A syntax extension that lets you write HTML-like code in JavaScript
questions.explanation: JSX compiles down to React.createElement calls.

questions.questionText: Why does React use a Virtual DOM?
options: To efficiently calculate minimal real DOM updates by diffing virtual trees
questions.explanation: Diffing virtual trees lets React batch and minimize expensive real DOM operations.

questions.questionText: What must a JSX expression always have at its root?
options: Multiple sibling elements without a wrapper
questions.explanation: JSX must return one enclosing element or a Fragment (<>...</>).

questions.questionText: How do you embed a JavaScript expression inside JSX?
questions.explanation: Curly braces let you drop any JS expression into JSX markup.

questions.questionText: What does calling the setter function returned by useState do?
options: Immediately mutates the variable and re-renders synchronously
Schedules a state update and triggers a re-render
questions.explanation: State updates are scheduled, not synchronous, and cause React to re-render the component.

questions.questionText: What is returned by useState(initialValue)?
questions.explanation: useState returns a two-element array, conventionally destructured as [value, setValue].

questions.questionText: Why shouldn't you mutate state directly (e.g. array.push()) in React?
options: It's fine to mutate as long as the component re-renders
React relies on new references to detect changes and re-render correctly
It throws a runtime error immediately
questions.explanation: React compares references to decide whether to re-render, so mutating in place can be missed.

questions.questionText: If you call the state setter with the same value as the current state, what typically happens?
options: React may bail out of re-rendering since the value hasn't changed
questions.explanation: React can skip a re-render when the new state is Object.is-equal to the old state.

questions.questionText: When does a useEffect with an empty dependency array [] run?
options: Only once, after the initial render
questions.explanation: An empty dependency array means the effect has no reactive values, so it runs once after mount.

questions.questionText: What is the purpose of the cleanup function returned from useEffect?
options: To clean up subscriptions/timers before the effect re-runs or the component unmounts
questions.explanation: The cleanup function prevents leaks like dangling timers or subscriptions.

questions.questionText: If you omit the dependency array entirely from useEffect, when does it run?
questions.explanation: No dependency array means the effect re-runs after every render.

questions.questionText: Which is a common use case for useEffect?
options: Fetching data from an API when the component mounts
questions.explanation: Data fetching is a classic side effect handled in useEffect.

questions.questionText: Which component is used to define a client-side route in React Router v6?
questions.explanation: <Route> maps a path to an element to render.

questions.questionText: What does the <Link> component do that a plain <a> tag doesn't?
options: Navigates without a full page reload
Opens links in a new tab by default
questions.explanation: <Link> updates the URL and view client-side, avoiding a full page reload.

questions.questionText: How do you access URL parameters (e.g. /users/:id) in a component?
questions.explanation: useParams returns an object of the route's dynamic segments.

questions.questionText: What does useNavigate() return?
options: A function to programmatically navigate to a new route
questions.explanation: Calling the function returned by useNavigate() pushes a new entry onto the history stack.

questions.questionText: What does app.get('/users', handler) define?
options: A route that responds to GET requests at /users
questions.explanation: app.get registers a handler for GET requests to that path.

questions.questionText: What object gives you access to the incoming request's data (body, params, query)?
questions.explanation: req represents the incoming HTTP request and carries its data.

questions.questionText: How do you define a route parameter in Express?
questions.explanation: A colon prefix marks a segment as a named route parameter.

questions.questionText: What does res.json(data) do?
options: Sends a JSON response and sets the Content-Type header
questions.explanation: res.json serializes data and sends it with the correct Content-Type.

questions.questionText: What is Express middleware?
options: A function with access to req, res, and next that can run code before the final handler
questions.explanation: Middleware functions form a pipeline that requests pass through.

questions.questionText: What happens if a middleware function doesn't call next() or send a response?
questions.explanation: Without next() or a response, the request never completes.

questions.questionText: Where is error-handling middleware typically placed in an Express app?
options: After all other routes and middleware
It doesn't need a specific place
questions.explanation: Error middleware must be registered last so errors from earlier handlers reach it.

questions.questionText: Which of these is an example of built-in Express middleware?
questions.explanation: express.json() is built into Express for parsing JSON request bodies.

questions.questionText: What are the three parts of a JWT?
questions.explanation: A JWT is three base64url segments joined by dots: header.payload.signature.

questions.questionText: Where is a JWT commonly sent from the client on subsequent requests?
options: In the Authorization header as a Bearer token
In a cookie only, never a header
questions.explanation: The Authorization: Bearer <token> header is the standard convention.

questions.questionText: What does hashing a password with bcrypt before storing it protect against?
options: Exposure of the plaintext password if the database is compromised
questions.explanation: Even if the database leaks, attackers only get the hash, not the original password.

questions.questionText: Is a JWT's payload encrypted by default?
options: No, it's base64-encoded and readable, but its signature prevents tampering
No, and the signature can be forged easily by anyone
questions.explanation: Anyone can decode a JWT payload; the signature only guarantees it hasn't been altered.

questions.questionText: What is the basic unit of data storage in MongoDB called?
questions.explanation: MongoDB stores data as documents, grouped into collections.

questions.questionText: What is a collection in MongoDB analogous to in a relational database?
questions.explanation: A collection groups documents the way a table groups rows.

questions.questionText: What format does MongoDB store documents in internally?
questions.explanation: BSON is a binary-encoded superset of JSON used internally by MongoDB.

questions.questionText: Which of these correctly finds documents where age is greater than 18?
questions.explanation: MongoDB uses query operators like $gt inside the filter object.

questions.questionText: What is a Mongoose Schema used for?
options: Defining the shape and validation rules of documents in a collection
questions.explanation: A Schema defines fields, types, and validation for documents.

questions.questionText: What does mongoose.model('User', userSchema) do?
options: Compiles a schema into a usable Model bound to the "users" collection
questions.explanation: Models are the interface used to create, query, and update documents.

questions.questionText: Which Mongoose feature lets you run code automatically before saving a document (e.g. hashing a password)?
questions.explanation: A pre('save') hook runs custom logic just before a document is persisted.

questions.questionText: What does .populate() do in Mongoose?
options: Fills empty fields with default values
Replaces a referenced ObjectId with the actual document it points to
questions.explanation: populate() resolves references to their full documents, similar to a join.

questions.questionText: What does Big-O notation describe?
options: The upper bound growth rate of an algorithm's time or space as input size grows
The number of lines of code
questions.explanation: Big-O describes how resource usage scales with input size, not exact timing.

questions.questionText: What is the time complexity of accessing an element in an array by index?
questions.explanation: Array indexing is a direct memory offset calculation, constant time.

questions.questionText: What is the time complexity of a simple nested loop over an array of size n?
questions.explanation: A loop inside a loop, each running n times, gives n * n operations.

questions.questionText: Which of these complexities grows the slowest as n increases?
questions.explanation: Logarithmic growth is far slower than linear, quadratic, or exponential growth.

questions.questionText: What is the time complexity of inserting an element at the beginning of an array?
questions.explanation: Every existing element must shift by one position, taking linear time.

questions.questionText: What is a common technique to check if a string is a palindrome efficiently?
options: Using two pointers from opposite ends comparing characters
Converting to an array of numbers
questions.explanation: Two pointers moving inward compare characters in linear time.

questions.questionText: Strings in JavaScript are:
questions.explanation: String operations always return a new string rather than modifying the original.

questions.questionText: What does 'hello'.split('') do in JavaScript?
options: Converts the string into an array of its characters
questions.explanation: Splitting on an empty string breaks it into an array of individual characters.

questions.questionText: What is the main advantage of a linked list over an array for insertion?
options: Insertion/deletion at a known position can be O(1) without shifting elements
questions.explanation: Linked lists avoid the shifting cost that arrays incur on insertion.

questions.questionText: What does each node in a singly linked list typically contain?
options: Data and a reference/pointer to the next node
questions.explanation: A singly linked list node holds its value plus a pointer to the next node.

questions.questionText: What is the time complexity of accessing the nth element in a singly linked list?
questions.explanation: You must traverse from the head, one node at a time, to reach the nth node.

questions.questionText: What distinguishes a doubly linked list from a singly linked list?
options: Each node has pointers to both the next and previous nodes
It stores data in an array
questions.explanation: Doubly linked lists allow traversal in both directions via next and prev pointers.

questions.questionText: What is the average time complexity of Quick Sort?
questions.explanation: Quick Sort averages O(n log n), though its worst case is O(n^2).

questions.questionText: Which sorting algorithm repeatedly divides the array in half, sorts each half, then merges them?
questions.explanation: Merge Sort is a classic divide-and-conquer algorithm.

questions.questionText: What is the worst-case time complexity of Bubble Sort?
questions.explanation: Bubble Sort's repeated full passes give it quadratic worst-case time.

questions.questionText: Which property describes a sorting algorithm that preserves the relative order of equal elements?
questions.explanation: A stable sort never reorders elements that compare as equal.

questions.questionText: What does encapsulation mean in OOP?
options: Bundling data and methods together while restricting direct access to internal state
Allowing a class to inherit from multiple classes
questions.explanation: Encapsulation hides internal state behind a controlled interface.

questions.questionText: What is polymorphism?
options: The ability of different classes to be treated through a common interface, typically by overriding methods
Converting a class into a function
questions.explanation: Polymorphism lets objects of different types respond to the same method call in their own way.

questions.questionText: What is inheritance in OOP?
options: A mechanism where a class derives properties and behavior from another class
questions.explanation: Inheritance lets a subclass reuse and extend a parent class's behavior.

questions.questionText: What does abstraction mean in OOP?
options: Hiding complex implementation details and exposing only essential features
questions.explanation: Abstraction simplifies interaction by hiding unnecessary complexity behind a clean interface.

questions.questionText: What does git commit do?
options: Saves a snapshot of staged changes to the local repository history
questions.explanation: A commit is a local snapshot; pushing is a separate step that uploads it.

questions.questionText: What is the difference between git merge and git rebase?
options: Merge combines histories with a merge commit; rebase replays commits onto a new base for a linear history
Rebase is only for deleting branches
Merge deletes the source branch automatically
questions.explanation: Merge preserves both histories with a merge commit; rebase rewrites commit history to be linear.

questions.questionText: What does git clone do?
options: Copies a remote repository to your local machine
questions.explanation: Cloning downloads the full repository history to a new local copy.

questions.questionText: What is a merge conflict?
options: When Git cannot automatically reconcile differing changes to the same lines and needs manual resolution
questions.explanation: Conflicts happen when two branches change the same lines differently and Git can't auto-resolve it.

questions.questionText: Which tag is the semantically correct choice for a page's main navigation links?
questions.explanation: <nav> explicitly marks a block of navigation links.

questions.questionText: Why use semantic HTML tags like <article> and <header> instead of generic <div>s?
options: They improve accessibility and SEO by conveying meaning about content structure
They are required for CSS to work
questions.explanation: Semantic tags give assistive technology and search engines structural meaning.

questions.questionText: Which tag represents a self-contained piece of content that could be distributed independently, like a blog post?
questions.explanation: <article> is meant for standalone, independently distributable content.

questions.questionText: What is the purpose of the alt attribute on an tag?
options: To provide alternative text for accessibility and when the image fails to load
questions.explanation: alt text is read by screen readers and shown if the image can't load.

questions.questionText: In Flexbox, which property controls the direction items are laid out (row vs column)?
questions.explanation: flex-direction sets the main axis as row or column.

questions.questionText: In CSS Grid, which property defines the number and size of columns?
questions.explanation: grid-template-columns explicitly defines the column tracks.

questions.questionText: Which Flexbox property aligns items along the main axis?
questions.explanation: justify-content controls alignment along the main axis; align-items handles the cross axis.

questions.questionText: When would you typically prefer CSS Grid over Flexbox?
options: For complex two-dimensional layouts (rows and columns simultaneously)
Grid always replaces Flexbox, never use Flexbox
questions.explanation: Grid excels at two-dimensional layouts; Flexbox is better suited to one-dimensional flows.
